dialogue.utterance: Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I have chest pain. It woke me up this morning.
Was the pain sharp, dull, or pressure-like, and did it radiate anywhere?
It's a dull feeling in my chest and radiates to my left arm.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Can you move your fingers and toes for us?
I can move them all.
Airway is patent.
Breathing rate is 16, regular rate and rhythm. Breath sounds are equal and clear (BBS clear bilaterally).
Radial pulse is strong, equal, and regular. Skin is warm, dry, and has normal color. No bleeding noted.
Initial pulse is 62 per minute.
Initial blood pressure is 202 over 90.
Respiration is 16 per minute, even and normal.
Oxygen saturation is 95%.
Blood glucose is 147 mg/dL.
EKG is showing a paced rhythm due to a cardiac pacemaker in the left upper chest.
Has anything made the pain better or worse?
It's been around all morning. I've felt this way before, but I can't remember exactly when.
In addition to the chest pain, have you experienced any nausea, vomiting, or other unusual symptoms?
No, just the chest discomfort.
On a scale of 1 to 10, with 10 being the worst pain you've ever had, how would you rate the pain now?
It's about a 5.
You are alert and oriented to time, place, person, and situation (AOx4). GCS is 15. You seem calm and cooperative.
You have a history of coronary artery disease and hypertension, as well as a cardiac pacemaker.
You're currently taking clopidogrel for your medical conditions.
You have no reported drug allergies.
Physical exam shows normal chest rise and fall; no chest wall tenderness or discoloration noted. No DCAP-BTLS seen.
We've obtained a 12-lead ECG showing ST and T wave abnormality consistent with anterolateral and inferior ischemia, as well as left ventricular hypertrophy.
A 4-lead cardiac monitor is in place, as per protocol.
We've started an 18-gauge IV in the left antecubital for medication administration.

Calling hospital now with a pre-arrival report: 89-year-old female with chest pain, history of CAD, hypertension, and a pacemaker; IV in left arm, 12-lead showing STT abnormalities, being transported in semi-Fowler's.
We are positioning you in a semi-Fowler's position for transport.
We will keep you in this position for the remainder of the ride.
Pulse is 81 beats per minute now.
Blood pressure is 167 over 89.
Respiration is still 16, even and normal.
Oxygen saturation remains at 98%.
Tried again, but still could not obtain glucose reading.
EKG remains in paced rhythm.
We are securing the patient onto the stretcher in semi-Fowler's position with shoulder and lap belts for transport.
We are transporting to VCU Health Systems as the resuscitation bay is prepared.
The patient remains alert and oriented with intact memory for time and location.
Do you know the year and location?
2019, somewhere near Richmond.
What did you do before we arrived?
I was sitting here on the couch.
Normocephalic skull, no visible facial deformity or injury.
No signs of fluid draining from ears or nasal cavity noted.
Oral mucosa is dry as per patient's report; unable to confirm hydration status fully.
No jugular venous distension; neck movements are within normal limits.
No murmurs, rubs, or gallops heard during auscultation.
Abdomen is soft, nontender with bowel sounds not fully evaluated due to time constraints.
Bilateral peripheral pulses are strong and equal. No cyanosis or swelling observed.
Skin is warm and dry with no visible pallor observed.
Capillary refill is less than 2 seconds.
Skin does not suggest signs of infection at this time.
We are maintaining the semi-Fowler's position for transport.
ECG changes continue to suggest ischemia; further evaluation at the hospital is necessary.
IV access remains patent and ready for use if necessary.
Readied stretcher and secured patient in semi-Fowler's with all monitoring in place.
Transport is now in progress to VCU Health Systems.
Arrival time logged and transfer of care awaits completion.
Final condition reported as stable with normal vitals at transition time.
Confirmed facility contacts and pre-arrival communication was followed correctly.
Summary of care and transport delivered verbally on arrival.
We are following protocol 2-1 for suspected cardiac chest pain and monitoring accordingly.
Full team coordination ensured during the transport.
Pre-transport checklist is complete.
Ambient temperature in the transport environment is acceptable.
Transport duration will be recorded for later review.
All necessary documentation and data collected to close incident.
Final sign off was executed after equipment and policy compliance.
Patient transport was handled safely, following all protocol steps.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light (PERRL), bilaterally 3mm.
Patient care is now being transferred to the staff at the receiving facility.
Occlusion during EKG due to patient's pacemaker requires technician at hospital for full clarification.
Medical summary and notes were left with receiving personnel for continuity.
Notes on clinical findings and transport conditions shared for follow-up care.
Handover completed and no outstanding procedures remain.
Patient is prepared for medical evaluation upon arrival.
Hospital staff have been advised of the patient's clinical status.
Skin turgor appears normal with no signs of dehydration observed.
The patient opens her eyes spontaneously.
The patient responds clearly and appropriately to questions.
The patient exhibits full motor response, following all commands.
Eyes remain open throughout the assessment.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15; patient remains alert and oriented.
Continuous EKG monitoring shows consistent paced rhythm for the duration.
Positioning is maintained as per semi-Fowler's throughout transport.
All initial vitals have been documented and shared.
Vital signs remain stable during transit.
Vital signs recorded just prior to hospital arrival.
Glucose attempts unsuccessful again, no immediate intervention indicated.
No new EKG changes or rhythm alterations have been noted during transport.
Patient appears comfortable and no distress is currently observed.
All straps and restraints in place for safe transport as per protocol.
Final check of all equipment and medications before arrival.
Time events have been accurately tracked for proper documentation.
Observations from the crew confirm no incident or issue during transport.
All medical devices functioned without interruption.
Patient handover was completed with full staff awareness and no further delays.
